sources.notes: Statement and constraints checked: the task is identical to F1, but n <= 3e5, t <= 1e4, and sum n <= 3e5.

Official editorial, full splay-tree reference code, and the added linked-list small-to-large alternative checked. They confirm that one clue splits one minimal free region and that the unchanged regions should be preserved.
Cross-checked the laminar containment forest, direct-free length f[v], Catalan product, and reverse deletion where a removed pair merges its contribution into the nearest surviving parent using DSU.
Cross-checked the reverse-time rooted-tree interpretation: deleted pair vertices are grouped with their nearest ancestor whose pair remains known, and each group contributes one Catalan factor.
Derived the forward split and reverse merge formulas independently, then exhaustively matched reverse-DSU outputs against direct enumeration for every balanced sequence and clue order through n=5.

annotation.summary: Answer the same incremental matching-pair completion counts as F1, but for up to 3e5 pairs in total, so all Catalan regions cannot be rescanned after each clue.
annotation.constraints: t <= 10000, 2 <= n <= 3e5, sum n <= 3e5; all n clues are distinct and compatible, and answers are modulo 998244353.
annotation.core_idea: Use the same laminar-region invariant as F1: a current free region containing 2k positions contributes Catalan(k). Adding a known pair consumes its two endpoints and splits one region into inside/outside regions of half-sizes a and b with a+b+1=k, so only Cat(k) is replaced by Cat(a)Cat(b). A simple hard-version solution is offline: use all n final pairs plus a virtual outer pair to build the containment tree by scanning endpoints with a stack. Start from the fully known state (answer 1) and delete clues in reverse. If v is deleted and p is its nearest still-known ancestor, their current region half-sizes x and y merge to x+y+1; update the product by Cat(x+y+1)/(Cat(x)Cat(y)). Union v into p, and DSU path compression skips ancestors already deleted. This recovers every prefix answer in reverse order.
annotation.complexity: Recommended offline solution: O(n alpha(n)) time and O(n) memory per test after O(Nmax) Catalan/inverse preprocessing. Official online splay and linked-list small-to-large variants are O(n log n) amortized time and O(n) memory.
tricks: Carry the exact F1 product invariant forward instead of treating official tags as the solution.
Reverse all updates so a difficult region split becomes a one-way merge into the nearest surviving ancestor.
Store region size in bracket-pair units: deleting v merges x and y into x+y+1 because v's two endpoints become one additional unknown pair.
Use DSU to jump over already deleted ancestors in the static laminar containment tree.
Alternatively maintain each free region as an implicit sequence and split/concatenate it online.

solution_variants.name: offline-reverse-containment-dsu
solution_variants.summary: Build the full matching-pair containment tree with a virtual root. Traverse clues backward; deleting vertex v merges its accumulated unknown-pair count into find(parent[v]), updates exactly two old Catalan factors into one new factor, and unions v upward.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n alpha(n)) time and O(n) memory after linear preprocessing.

solution_variants.name: online-implicit-splay-region-split
solution_variants.summary: Represent every current free region as one implicit splay-tree component ordered by position. Splay l and r, cut out the between-endpoints subsequence, concatenate the outside prefix and suffix, remove l/r from the free region, and update subtree sizes and Catalan factors.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) amortized time and O(n) memory.

solution_variants.name: online-small-to-large-linked-regions
solution_variants.summary: Maintain free-region position lists and their region owners. When l,r split a list, walk both resulting sides in parallel, relabel only the smaller side, retain both region sizes, and update the same Catalan split ratio; every position is relabeled O(log n) times.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) amortized time and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: Every current free region contributes Cat(size/2), and every update is exactly a replacement or merge of Catalan factors.
All good-pair intervals are nested or disjoint, yielding the static containment tree on which reverse merging is performed.
The recommended solution reads all clues, builds their final containment tree, and processes deletions in reverse to replace splits by merges.
After deleting a pair, unioning it into its parent lets find(parent[v]) return the nearest ancestor whose pair is still known.
A deletion changes only Cat(size[v]) and Cat(size[parent]) into Cat(size[v]+size[parent]+1); all other region factors remain untouched.
The official online solution keeps each free region as an ordered implicit sequence supporting cut, split, concatenate, and subtree-size queries.

tags.description: A self-adjusting binary search tree, including implicit-key forms used to expose positions and split or concatenate dynamic sequences in amortized logarithmic time.
tags.created_reason: The official online F2 solution specifically splays endpoints to component roots before cutting and joining region sequences; the existing implicit-sequence tag describes the represented object but not this classic balancing structure. Candidate for main-agent taxonomy review.
tags.evidence: The official implementation uses rotations and splay operations to expose l and r, detach three subsequences, join the outside pieces, and maintain component sizes.
The official alternative relabels only the smaller side after each linked-list split, so each position changes region ownership only O(log n) times.
An endpoint scan with a stack builds the full laminar containment tree for the offline solution.
Known pairs plus a virtual outer pair form a rooted containment tree, and reverse updates merge vertices toward surviving ancestors.
Both official hard-version variants can process clues in their original order while immediately maintaining the region product.
The counted structures and the laminar matching relation are those of regular bracket sequences.
